title: Descriptive statistics of a sample
prompt:
A batch of **20 turned shafts** was measured; the diameters (in **µm** above nominal) are listed in column **A** (cells **A2:A21**). Compute the standard descriptive statistics of the sample in column **C** using the Excel functions.

These are the very functions you must know cold for the exam: $\text{AVERAGE}$, $\text{MEDIAN}$, $\text{MODE}$, $\text{VAR.S}$, $\text{STDEV.S}$, $\text{MIN}$, $\text{MAX}$, $\text{COUNT}$ and $\text{QUARTILE}$.
hint: Sample (not population) statistics divide by $n-1$: use VAR.S / STDEV.S, not VAR.P / STDEV.P.

given.A1: Diameter (µm)

checks.label: Average
checks.teach: $\text{AVERAGE}$ returns the arithmetic mean $\bar{x}=\frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$. Here $\bar{x}=890/20=44.5$ µm. It is the balance point of the data and the first thing you report about any sample.

checks.label: Median
checks.teach: The $\text{MEDIAN}$ is the middle value once the data are sorted (the average of the two central values when $n$ is even). Unlike the mean it is robust to outliers — when median and mean coincide, the sample is roughly symmetric.

checks.label: Mode
checks.teach: $\text{MODE}$ (identical to $\text{MODE.SNGL}$ in newer Excel) returns the most frequent value — here 45 µm appears five times, more than any other reading.

checks.label: Sample variance
checks.teach: $\text{VAR.S}$ is the **sample** variance $s^2=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2$, dividing by $n-1=19$ (Bessel's correction). Use it whenever the data are a sample of a larger population — which is the normal case in metrology.

checks.label: Sample standard deviation
checks.teach: $\text{STDEV.S}=\sqrt{\text{VAR.S}}$ — the spread of the data in the same units as the measurement. $s\approx1.61$ µm summarises the dispersion of the diameters.

checks.label: Minimum
checks.teach: $\text{MIN}$ returns the smallest reading (42 µm). Together with MAX it gives the **range** $= \text{MAX}-\text{MIN}$, a quick measure of total spread.

checks.label: Maximum
checks.teach: $\text{MAX}$ returns the largest reading (48 µm). The range here is $48-42=6$ µm.

checks.label: Count
checks.teach: $\text{COUNT}$ counts the **numeric** cells, giving the sample size $n=20$. (Use $\text{COUNTA}$ if you also need to count text cells.)

checks.label: First quartile Q1
checks.teach: $\text{QUARTILE}(\text{range};1)$ is the 25th-percentile $Q_1$: 25 % of the data lie below it. The interquartile range $Q_3-Q_1$ is a robust spread measure.

checks.label: Third quartile Q3
checks.teach: $\text{QUARTILE}(\text{range};3)$ is the 75th-percentile $Q_3$. The argument selects which quartile: 0 = min, 1 = $Q_1$, 2 = median, 3 = $Q_3$, 4 = max.

method: Type each function over the data range $A2:A21$. The mean ($44.5$) and median ($44.5$) coincide, so the sample is near-symmetric; the mode ($45$) is the most repeated reading. Use the **sample** forms VAR.S / STDEV.S (divide by $n-1$) because the 20 shafts are a sample of the whole production.

title: Normal distribution — probability in an interval
prompt:
A dimension is normally distributed with mean **m = 85** and standard deviation **s = 1.2** (same units). Using **NORM.DIST(x; m; s; cumulative)** with the cumulative flag **TRUE (1)** — which returns the area to the left of $x$, i.e. $P(X\le x)$ — answer the three questions.

A probability over an interval is the **difference of two CDF values**: $P(a<X<b)=F(b)-F(a)$.
hint: P(X>x) = 1 − NORM.DIST(x;…;1). For an interval, subtract the two cumulative values.

given.A1: Normal distribution

checks.label: P(X < 83.6)
checks.teach: Cumulative = 1 gives the left-tail area $P(X\le 83.6)=F(83.6)=0.1217$. This is the standard way to read a 'less than' probability straight off the Normal CDF.

checks.label: P(X > 86.2)
checks.teach: Excel only gives the left tail, so the **right** tail is the complement: $P(X>86.2)=1-F(86.2)=1-0.8413=0.1587$. (Here 86.2 is exactly $+1s$ above the mean, hence the classic 0.1587.)

checks.label: P(85.4 < X < 86.2)
checks.teach: Probability over an interval = $F(86.2)-F(85.4)=0.8413-0.6306=0.2108$. Always subtract the lower CDF from the upper one; never add them.

method: All three use NORM.DIST with cumulative = 1. 'Less than' is $F(x)$ directly; 'greater than' is $1-F(x)$; an interval is $F(b)-F(a)$.

title: Standardisation and the standard normal
prompt:
The same dimension is $X\sim N(85,\,1.2)$. Standardising converts any value to a $z$-score $z=\dfrac{x-m}{s}$, after which you may use the **standard** normal **NORM.S.DIST(z; cumulative)** (no mean/σ needed).

Compute the $z$-score of $x=83.6$ in **C5**, then the left-tail probability $P(Z\le z)$ two ways — once with NORM.S.DIST and once with the full NORM.DIST — and check they agree.
hint: z = (x − m)/s. Then NORM.S.DIST(z;1) must equal NORM.DIST(x;m;s;1).

given.A1: Standardising x = 83.6

checks.label: z-score
checks.teach: Standardising shifts and scales: $z=\frac{83.6-85}{1.2}=-1.1667$. A negative $z$ means the value lies below the mean, by $1.17$ standard deviations.

checks.label: P(Z ≤ z) standard
checks.teach: NORM.S.DIST takes only $z$ and the cumulative flag because the standard normal has $m=0,\,s=1$. With cumulative = 1 it returns $P(Z\le z)=0.1217$.

checks.label: P(X ≤ x) full
checks.teach: The full NORM.DIST on the raw value gives the **same** 0.1217 — proof that standardising then using the standard normal is equivalent to using the general normal directly.

method: Standardise with $z=(x-m)/s=-1.1667$; then $P=\Phi(z)=0.1217$. The two routes agree exactly, which is the whole point of the $z$-transform.

title: Inverse normal — the value not exceeded with a given probability
prompt:
For $X\sim N(85,\,1.2)$ we now go the **other way**: given a probability, find the value. Use the inverse functions **NORM.INV(P; m; s)** (returns the $x$ with $P(X\le x)=P$) and **NORM.S.INV(P)** (returns the corresponding $z$).

Find the **lower boundary** $x_i$ leaving a left-tail risk $\alpha_i=0.01$, the **upper boundary** $x_s$ leaving a right-tail risk $\alpha_s=0.01$, and the value **not exceeded with probability 0.95**.
hint: Upper boundary uses 1 − α: NORM.INV(1−αs; m; s). 'Not exceeded with prob. 0.95' is simply NORM.INV(0.95; m; s).

given.A1: Inverse normal, X ~ N(85, 1.2)

checks.label: Lower boundary xi
checks.teach: NORM.INV inverts the CDF: feeding the cumulative probability $\alpha_i=0.01$ returns $x_i=82.21$, the value below which only 1 % of the population lies.

checks.label: Upper boundary xs
checks.teach: For an **upper** boundary you must pass the cumulative probability to its left, namely $1-\alpha_s=0.99$, so $x_s=\text{NORM.INV}(0.99;85;1.2)=87.79$. Passing $\alpha_s$ directly is the classic mistake.

checks.label: Value not exceeded with prob. 0.95
checks.teach: 'Not exceeded with probability 0.95' means $P(X\le x)=0.95$, so $x=\text{NORM.INV}(0.95;85;1.2)=86.97$ — the 95th percentile of the dimension.

checks.label: z for 0.95 quantile
checks.teach: NORM.S.INV returns the **standard** quantile $z_{0.95}=1.645$. Check: $m+z\,s=85+1.645\times1.2=86.97$, matching C8 — the general inverse equals mean plus $z$ times σ.

method: Inverse problems map a probability back to a value. Left boundary: NORM.INV(α). Right boundary: NORM.INV(1−α). '95th percentile' = NORM.INV(0.95). The standard quantile $z_{0.95}=1.645$ links the two: $x=m+z\,s$.

title: Confidence interval for the mean (Student t)
prompt:
Ten repeated measurements of a shaft diameter (mm) are in column **B** (**B2:B11**). The population standard deviation is **unknown**, so the confidence interval for the mean uses the **Student t** distribution.

With $\bar{x}=\text{AVERAGE}$, sample std. dev. $s=\text{STDEV.S}$, $n=\text{COUNT}$ and significance $\alpha=0.05$, the half-width is
$$h = t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-1}\,\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}},\qquad t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-1}=\text{T.INV.2T}(\alpha;\,n-1).$$
Compute $\bar{x}$, $s$, $n$, the critical $t$, the half-width $h$, and the two interval bounds $\bar{x}\mp h$.
hint: T.INV.2T(α; n−1) already gives the two-tailed critical value t_{1−α/2}. Half-width = t·s/√n.

given.A1: Diameter (mm)

checks.label: Sample mean
checks.teach: $\bar{x}=\text{AVERAGE}(B2{:}B11)=50.05$ mm is the point estimate the interval is built around.

checks.label: Sample std. dev.
checks.teach: $s=\text{STDEV.S}=0.3028$ mm. Because $\sigma$ is unknown we estimate it from the sample — this is exactly why the interval needs the $t$ distribution, not the normal.

checks.label: Sample size
checks.teach: $n=\text{COUNT}=10$, giving $n-1=9$ degrees of freedom for the $t$ distribution.

checks.label: Critical t (two-tailed)
checks.teach: $\text{T.INV.2T}(\alpha;n-1)$ returns the **two-tailed** critical value directly: $t_{1-\alpha/2,\,9}=2.262$. Equivalently $\text{T.INV}(1-\alpha/2;\,n-1)$ gives the same number.

checks.label: Half-width h
checks.teach: $h=t\cdot s/\sqrt{n}=2.262\times0.3028/\sqrt{10}=0.2166$ mm. The $/\sqrt{n}$ converts the spread of the **data** into the spread of the **sample mean** (the standard error).

checks.label: Lower bound
checks.teach: Lower confidence limit $\bar{x}-h=50.05-0.2166=49.83$ mm.

checks.label: Upper bound
checks.teach: Upper confidence limit $\bar{x}+h=50.27$ mm. We are 95 % confident the true mean diameter lies in $[49.83,\,50.27]$ mm.

method: Estimate $\bar{x}=50.05$, $s=0.3028$, $n=10$. The two-tailed critical value is $t=\text{T.INV.2T}(0.05;9)=2.262$. Half-width $h=t\,s/\sqrt{n}=0.217$ mm. The 95 % confidence interval for the mean is $\bar{x}\mp h=[49.83,\,50.27]$ mm.

title: Two normals — risk of error of the second kind β
prompt:
A production process is **normal** with mean $m=10.25$ and standard deviation $s=0.12$. A proposed modification would shift it to $m_1=10.63$, $s_1=0.09$. With confidence level $P=0.95$ we accept a part as 'unchanged' if it falls in the **original** confidence interval. Evaluate the probability that a part actually from the **new** distribution still lands in that interval — the **risk of error of the second kind β**.

Build the original interval $[L,U]$ with NORM.INV, then compute $\beta=F_1(U)-F_1(L)$ using the **new** distribution's CDF (NORM.DIST with $m_1,s_1$).
hint: Limits: NORM.INV((1−P)/2; m; s) and NORM.INV(1−(1−P)/2; m; s). Then β = NORM.DIST(U;m1;s1;1) − NORM.DIST(L;m1;s1;1).

given.A1: β: a 'new' part falling inside the old interval
given.A7: Lower limit L of original interval
given.A8: Upper limit U of original interval

checks.label: Lower limit L
checks.teach: A symmetric two-sided interval at confidence $P$ leaves $(1-P)/2$ in each tail. The lower limit is the $(1-P)/2=0.025$ quantile of the **original** normal: $L=\text{NORM.INV}(0.025;10.25;0.12)=10.0148$.

checks.label: Upper limit U
checks.teach: The upper limit is the $1-(1-P)/2=0.975$ quantile: $U=\text{NORM.INV}(0.975;10.25;0.12)=10.4852$. So the original 95 % interval is $[10.015,\,10.485]$.

checks.label: Risk β
checks.teach: β is the probability that a part from the **new** distribution still falls in the old interval: $\beta=F_1(U)-F_1(L)$ using $m_1,s_1$. Here $\beta=0.0538$ — about a 5 % chance of failing to detect the modification. Note you take the difference of CDFs of the **new** distribution over the **old** limits.

method: Original 95 % interval: $L=\text{NORM.INV}(0.025;10.25;0.12)=10.0148$, $U=\text{NORM.INV}(0.975;10.25;0.12)=10.4852$. Then $\beta=F_1(U)-F_1(L)$ with the new $N(10.63,0.09)$: $\beta=0.0538$. This is the type-II error — accepting the unchanged hypothesis when the process has actually moved.

title: Bilateral test on a repaired machine (normal mean)
prompt:
A machine makes parts with mass $\sim N(\mu=351\,\text{g},\,\cdot)$. After a repair, a sample of **n = 50** parts gives mean **mx = 340 g** and sample std. dev. **sx = 20 g**. Test, at risk $\alpha=0.05$ **bilateral**, whether the repair changed the production.

The sample mean has standard deviation (standard error) $s_{\bar{x}}=s_x/\sqrt{n}$. Build the 95 % acceptance interval for $\bar{x}$ centred on $mx$ with NORM.INV, then decide whether $\mu=351$ falls **outside** it (reject) or inside (do not reject).
hint: Standard error = sx/√n. Limits = NORM.INV(α/2; mx; SE) and NORM.INV(1−α/2; mx; SE). Reject if μ is outside.

given.A1: Bilateral test on the repaired machine

checks.label: Standard error
checks.teach: The spread of the **sample mean** is the standard error $s_{\bar{x}}=s_x/\sqrt{n}=20/\sqrt{50}=2.828$ g — much smaller than the single-part spread of 20 g, because averaging reduces variability.

checks.label: Lower limit
checks.teach: Lower bound of the bilateral acceptance interval: $\text{NORM.INV}(\alpha/2;mx;s_{\bar{x}})=\text{NORM.INV}(0.025;340;2.828)=334.46$ g.

checks.label: Upper limit
checks.teach: Upper bound: $\text{NORM.INV}(0.975;340;2.828)=345.54$ g. So the acceptance interval for the mean is $[334.46,\,345.54]$ g.

checks.label: Reject H0?
checks.teach: The hypothesised $\mu=351$ g lies **above** the upper limit 345.54 g, so it falls outside the 95 % interval — we **reject** $H_0$: the repair did change the production. The IF/OR returns 1.

method: Standard error $s_{\bar{x}}=20/\sqrt{50}=2.828$ g. Acceptance interval for the mean: $\text{NORM.INV}(0.025;340;2.828)=334.46$ to $\text{NORM.INV}(0.975;340;2.828)=345.54$ g. Since $\mu=351$ g is outside this interval, reject $H_0$ — the repair affected the mass.

title: Two supplies merged — hypergeometric acceptance
prompt:
A factory buys **NA = 5000** parts from supplier A with defectivity **DA = 0.01**, and **NB = 2000** parts from B with **DB = 0.02**. From the merged stock it draws a sample of **NC = 10** parts (without replacement). Find the probability that **no more than ND = 1** defective part is in the sample.

First compute the total number of defectives in the merged population, then use **HYPGEOM.DIST(k; n; C; M; cumulative)** with cumulative = 1.
hint: Total defectives C = NA·DA + NB·DB. Population M = NA + NB. Then HYPGEOM.DIST(1; NC; C; M; 1).

given.A1: Merged supplies — P(no more than 1 defective)
given.A8: Total defectives in merged stock  C =

checks.label: Total defectives C
checks.teach: Defectives add up across suppliers: $C=N_A D_A+N_B D_B=5000(0.01)+2000(0.02)=50+40=90$ in a merged population of $M=7000$.

checks.label: P(at most 1 defective)
checks.teach: Drawing **without replacement** from a finite stock → hypergeometric. With cumulative = 1, $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;10;90;7000;1)=0.9931$ sums $P(0)+P(1)$. The population size argument is $M=N_A+N_B=7000$.

method: Total defectives $C=N_AD_A+N_BD_B=90$ out of $M=7000$. 'No more than 1' is the cumulative hypergeometric at $k=1$: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;10;90;7000;1)=0.9931$.

title: Student t — tail probabilities and inverse
prompt:
A test statistic follows a **Student t** distribution with **ν = 7** degrees of freedom. Using **T.DIST(t; ν; cumulative)** (cumulative = 1 gives the left tail $P(T\le t)$) and the inverse **T.INV(P; ν)**, answer the questions.

Recall: a right tail is $1-F(t)$; the inverse maps a left-tail probability back to a $t$ value.
hint: P(T>t) = 1 − T.DIST(t;ν;1). Lower boundary = T.INV(αi;ν); upper boundary = T.INV(1−αs;ν).

given.A1: Student t, ν = 7 degrees of freedom

checks.label: P(T > 1.3)
checks.teach: T.DIST with cumulative = 1 gives the left tail; the right tail is the complement: $P(T>1.3)=1-F(1.3)=0.1174$. The t distribution has heavier tails than the normal, so this exceeds the normal's value.

checks.label: P(T < 2.6)
checks.teach: A 'less than' probability reads straight off the cumulative: $P(T<2.6)=F(2.6)=0.9823$ for $\nu=7$.

checks.label: Lower boundary ti
checks.teach: T.INV inverts the left-tail CDF. Feeding $\alpha_i=0.01$ returns the **negative** quantile $t_i=-2.998$, below which 1 % of the distribution lies. The t distribution is symmetric, so left quantiles are negative.

checks.label: Upper boundary ts
checks.teach: For an upper boundary leaving right-tail risk $\alpha_s=0.02$, pass the left-cumulative $1-\alpha_s=0.98$: $t_s=\text{T.INV}(0.98;7)=2.517$.

title: Separating two products — equal-risk limit mass
prompt:
Two product types have normal masses: $T_1\sim N(m_1=12.32,\,\sigma_1^2=0.25)$ and $T_2\sim N(m_2=13.48,\,\sigma_2^2=0.18)$ (masses in g, variances in g²). A part is assigned to $T_1$ if its mass is below a limit $m_c$, otherwise to $T_2$.

The correct $m_c$ makes the two misclassification risks **equal**. The solution file reports $m_c = 12.9475$ g. Verify the two risks at $m_c$ and that their **difference is essentially zero**.

Note the **standard deviations** are $\sqrt{\sigma^2}$, so pass SQRT of the variance to NORM.DIST.
hint: Risk of T1-into-T2 = 1 − NORM.DIST(mc; m1; SQRT(σ1²); 1). Risk of T2-into-T1 = NORM.DIST(mc; m2; SQRT(σ2²); 1). At the right mc they are equal → difference ≈ 0.

given.A1: Equal-risk separation limit
given.A7: Risk: a T1 part assigned to T2  (mass > mc)
given.A8: Risk: a T2 part assigned to T1  (mass < mc)

checks.label: Risk T1→T2
checks.teach: A $T_1$ part is wrongly sent to $T_2$ if its mass exceeds $m_c$: $P(X_1>m_c)=1-F_1(m_c)$ with $\sigma_1=\sqrt{0.25}=0.5$. This is the **right** tail of the lighter distribution.

checks.label: Risk T2→T1
checks.teach: A $T_2$ part is wrongly sent to $T_1$ if its mass is below $m_c$: $P(X_2<m_c)=F_2(m_c)$ with $\sigma_2=\sqrt{0.18}=0.424$. This is the **left** tail of the heavier distribution.

checks.label: Difference of risks
checks.teach: At the correct $m_c=12.9475$ g the two misclassification risks are equal, so their difference is essentially zero ($\approx-2\times10^{-5}$). That equal-risk condition is exactly what defines the optimal separation limit. Remember to convert each **variance** to a standard deviation with SQRT before calling NORM.DIST.

method: Convert variances to σ via SQRT ($\sigma_1=0.5$, $\sigma_2=0.424$). Risk $T_1\to T_2=1-F_1(m_c)=0.1047$; risk $T_2\to T_1=F_2(m_c)=0.1047$. They match at $m_c=12.9475$ g, so the difference is ≈ 0 — the defining equal-risk condition.

title: Risk of failure of a multi-part mechanism (hypergeometric)
prompt:
A mechanism is built from critical pieces of three types: **nA = 5** of type A, **nB = 3** of type B, **nC = 4** of type C. Each type is drawn from a batch of **500** units containing, respectively, **DA = 2**, **DB = 4**, **DC = 3** defective pieces. The mechanism **fails if even a single defective piece** is among its components.

It is far easier to compute the probability that **all** pieces are good and take the complement. For each type, the chance of drawing $n$ pieces with **zero** defectives from a batch of 500 is the hypergeometric mass at $k=0$: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(0;\,n;\,D;\,500;\,0)$. Multiply the three (independent batches) and the risk of failure is $1-$ that product.
hint: P(all good, type X) = HYPGEOM.DIST(0; nX; DX; 500; 0). Risk = 1 − PA·PB·PC.

given.A1: Risk of failure — three part types from batches of 500

checks.label: P(all A good)
checks.teach: Drawing $n_A=5$ pieces from 500 with $D_A=2$ defectives, the probability that **none** of the 5 is defective is the hypergeometric mass at $k=0$: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(0;5;2;500;0)=0.9801$. The last argument 0 (FALSE) selects the exact mass, not the cumulative.

checks.label: P(all B good)
checks.teach: For type B: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(0;3;4;500;0)=0.9761$. More defectives in the batch (4) but fewer pieces drawn (3).

checks.label: P(all C good)
checks.teach: For type C: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(0;4;3;500;0)=0.9761$ — coincidentally equal to type B here.

checks.label: Risk of failure
checks.teach: The mechanism works only if **all three** types are defect-free, and the three draws are independent, so $P(\text{works})=P_A P_B P_C$. The risk of failure is the complement $1-P_A P_B P_C=1-0.9339=0.0661$. Computing the complement is the trick: enumerating every way the mechanism could fail is far harder.

method: The mechanism fails on any single defective, so work with its complement — all pieces good. Per type, $P(\text{all good})=\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(0;n;D;500;0)$, giving $0.9801$, $0.9761$, $0.9761$. The product is the probability the whole mechanism works ($0.9339$); the **risk of failure** is $1-0.9339=0.0661$.

title: Acceptance sampling — risk of accepting a bad batch (binomial)
prompt:
A production split into large batches is inspected by drawing **n = 50** pieces from each batch. The batch is **accepted** if **no more than k = 1** piece is out of tolerance. For a batch whose true fraction out of tolerance is **P = 0.05**, find the **risk of accepting** it.

Because the batch is large, the count of out-of-tolerance pieces in the sample is **binomial**. 'No more than $k$' is the **cumulative** binomial $P(X\le k)=\text{BINOM.DIST}(k;\,n;\,P;\,1)$.
hint: Risk = BINOM.DIST(k; n; P; 1) with cumulative = 1 (TRUE).

given.A1: Risk of accepting a bad batch — single-phase plan
given.A5: Risk of accepting the batch  P(X ≤ k)

checks.label: Risk of acceptance
checks.teach: The sample contains $X$ out-of-tolerance pieces, $X\sim\text{Binomial}(n{=}50,\,P{=}0.05)$. The batch is accepted when $X\le 1$, so the acceptance risk is the **cumulative** binomial $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;50;0.05;1)=P(0)+P(1)=0.2794$. The cumulative flag 1 is what sums $P(0)$ and $P(1)$; with flag 0 you would get only $P(1)$.

method: Sample defect count is $X\sim\text{Binomial}(50,0.05)$. 'Accept if $\le 1$' makes the risk the cumulative $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;50;0.05;1)=0.2794$ — a 28 % chance of passing a batch that is actually 5 % defective. That high risk is exactly why acceptance plans must be designed deliberately (see S02).

title: Acceptance sampling — minimum sample size for a target risk
prompt:
Same single-phase plan with **k = 1** accepted defective and true fraction **P = 0.05**, but now you may choose the sample size. Find the **minimum number n** of pieces to inspect so that the risk of accepting such a batch is at most **R = 0.2**.

The risk decreases as $n$ grows, so increase $n$ until $\text{BINOM.DIST}(k;\,n;\,P;\,1)\le R$. The solution file reports **n = 59**: at $n=59$ the risk is $0.1991\le 0.2$, while $n=58$ still gives $0.2069>0.2$.
hint: Try increasing n in BINOM.DIST(k; n; P; 1) until it drops below R. The smallest such n is 59.

given.A1: Minimum sample size for an acceptable risk
given.A6: Risk at n = 59  (≤ R, so n is enough)
given.A7: Risk at n = 58  (> R, so 58 is too few)

checks.label: Minimum n
checks.teach: There is no closed-form inverse here, so you increase $n$ until the acceptance risk falls to the target. The boundary is sharp: at $n=58$ the risk is still $0.2069$ (above $R=0.2$), and at $n=59$ it drops to $0.1991$ (at or below $R$). So the **minimum** sample size is $n=59$. This is a pure-search answer — there is no single formula, you read it off the two checks below.

checks.label: Risk at n = 59
checks.teach: At $n=59$: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;59;0.05;1)=0.1991\le 0.2$. The condition is met, so 59 is sufficient.

checks.label: Risk at n = 58
checks.teach: At $n=58$: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;58;0.05;1)=0.2069>0.2$ — still above the target, which proves 58 pieces are too few. Together C6 and C7 bracket the minimum at $n=59$.

method: Increase $n$ until $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;n;0.05;1)\le 0.2$. Since the risk at $n=58$ is $0.2069>0.2$ and at $n=59$ is $0.1991\le 0.2$, the minimum sample size is $\boxed{n=59}$. There is no inverse function for this — it is a monotone search on the cumulative binomial.

title: Acceptance sampling from a finite batch (hypergeometric)
prompt:
Now each batch is **finite**: **M = 500** pieces, of which the fraction **P = 0.05** are out of tolerance. You draw **n = 50** pieces **without replacement** and accept if **no more than k = 1** is out of tolerance. Find the risk of acceptance.

Drawing without replacement from a finite batch makes the count **hypergeometric**, not binomial. First compute the number of defectives in the batch $C=P\cdot M$, then the acceptance risk is the cumulative hypergeometric $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(k;\,n;\,C;\,M;\,1)$. Compare with the binomial approximation.
hint: Defectives C = P·M. Risk = HYPGEOM.DIST(k; n; C; M; 1). Approx = BINOM.DIST(k; n; P; 1).

given.A1: Risk of acceptance — finite batch, drawn without replacement
given.A6: Defectives in the batch  C = P·M

checks.label: Defectives in batch C
checks.teach: The number of defective pieces actually present is $C=P\cdot M=0.05\times 500=25$. The hypergeometric needs this **count**, not the fraction.

checks.label: Risk (hypergeometric)
checks.teach: Without replacement from a finite batch, $X$ is hypergeometric. 'Accept if $\le 1$' is the cumulative $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;50;25;500;1)=0.2636$. The arguments are (successes $k$; sample $n$; population successes $C$; population $M$; cumulative).

checks.label: Risk (binomial approx)
checks.teach: If you (wrongly, but as a quick approximation) treat each draw as independent, the binomial gives $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;50;0.05;1)=0.2794$. It overestimates the risk here because sampling 50 from only 500 noticeably depletes the batch; the hypergeometric ($0.2636$) is the exact value. The binomial approaches the hypergeometric only when $n\ll M$.

method: Defectives present: $C=P\cdot M=25$. Exact risk (without replacement) is hypergeometric: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;50;25;500;1)=0.2636$. The binomial approximation $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;50;0.05;1)=0.2794$ is close but high because $n=50$ is a non-negligible fraction of $M=500$.

title: Two-phase acceptance plan — total acceptance probability
prompt:
A batch of **nl = 5000** pieces is inspected in **two phases**. Phase 1: inspect **nc1 = 50** pieces; if **nf1 = 0** are out of tolerance, accept immediately. If exactly one is found, go to phase 2: inspect **nc2 = 100** more; accept only if the **total** out of tolerance over both phases is **≤ nf2 = 1** (i.e. zero new defectives in phase 2). The true fraction out of tolerance is **p = 0.05**.

All counts are hypergeometric (without replacement). The batch carries $p\cdot nl=250$ defectives. Build the pieces of the acceptance probability and sum the two accepting paths.
hint: Defectives = p·nl = 250. Accept-on-phase-1 = HYPGEOM.DIST(0; nc1; 250; nl; 0). Phase-1 'exactly one' = HYPGEOM.DIST(1; nc1; 250; nl; 0); phase-2 'zero' = HYPGEOM.DIST(0; nc2; 249; nl−nc1; 0). Total = first path + (one)·(zero).

given.A1: Two-phase acceptance plan — total acceptance probability
given.A9: P1: phase-1 has exactly nf1 (=0) out of tol. → accept
given.A10: P1(1): phase-1 has exactly one out of tol. → go to phase 2
given.A11: P2(0): phase-2 has zero out of tol. (249 left, nl−nc1)
given.A12: P(both phases give total nf2): P1(1)·P2(0)
given.A13: Total risk of accepting the batch

checks.label: Defectives p·nl
checks.teach: The batch holds $p\cdot n_l=0.05\times 5000=250$ out-of-tolerance pieces. Every hypergeometric call below uses this count as the population successes.

checks.label: P1 (accept in phase 1)
checks.teach: Phase 1 accepts outright when **zero** of the 50 are out of tolerance: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(0;50;250;5000;0)=0.0760$. This is the first accepting path.

checks.label: P1(1) (go to phase 2)
checks.teach: Finding **exactly one** out of tolerance in phase 1 triggers the second phase: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;50;250;5000;0)=0.2020$.

checks.label: P2(0) (zero in phase 2)
checks.teach: Entering phase 2, one defective and 50 pieces have already been removed, so the remaining population is $n_l-n_{c1}=4950$ with $250-1=249$ defectives. The batch is accepted only with **zero** further defectives in the 100 inspected: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(0;100;249;4950;0)=0.00543$. Note both the population and its defective count are updated for the conditional second draw.

checks.label: Both-phase accept path
checks.teach: The second accepting path requires one defective in phase 1 **and** none in phase 2, so multiply the (conditionally independent) probabilities: $0.2020\times 0.00543=0.001098$.

checks.label: Total acceptance risk
checks.teach: The two accepting paths are mutually exclusive, so add them: outright acceptance ($0.0760$) plus the one-then-none path ($0.0011$) gives a total acceptance risk of $0.0771$. The extra phase only marginally raises the acceptance chance over phase 1 alone, but lets you control inspection cost.

method: Batch defectives $=p\cdot n_l=250$. Phase-1 outright accept: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(0;50;250;5000;0)=0.0760$. Go-to-phase-2 chance $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;50;250;5000;0)=0.2020$ times phase-2 clean $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(0;100;249;4950;0)=0.00543$ gives $0.0011$. Total acceptance $=0.0760+0.0011=0.0771$.

title: Acceptance probability — hypergeometric vs binomial difference
prompt: Batches of **Np = 1250** pieces are inspected by controlling **Na = 15** pieces; the batch is accepted if **no more than one** is out of tolerance. A particular batch contains **Nf = 22** out-of-tolerance pieces. Compute the acceptance probability with the **hypergeometric** (exact, without replacement) and the **binomial** (approximate) distributions, and report their **difference**.
hint: Hypergeometric: HYPGEOM.DIST(1; Na; Nf; Np; 1). Binomial: BINOM.DIST(1; Na; Nf/Np; 1). Difference = first − second.

given.A1: Acceptance probability — exact vs approximate
given.A4: out of tolerance in batch  Nf =

checks.label: Acceptance (hypergeometric)
checks.teach: Sampling 15 from a finite batch of 1250 without replacement → hypergeometric. 'At most one out of tolerance' is cumulative: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;15;22;1250;1)=0.97296$. This is the exact acceptance probability.

checks.label: Acceptance (binomial)
checks.teach: The binomial approximation treats each draw as independent with success probability $N_f/N_p=22/1250=0.0176$: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;15;0.0176;1)=0.97206$. Because $N_a=15\ll N_p=1250$, the batch is barely depleted and the approximation is excellent.

checks.label: Probability difference
checks.teach: The difference is only $0.97296-0.97206\approx 9\times10^{-4}$ — negligible. This quantifies the rule of thumb: when the sample is a small fraction of the population, the binomial is a safe stand-in for the hypergeometric.

method: Exact (hypergeometric): $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;15;22;1250;1)=0.97296$. Approximate (binomial, $p=22/1250$): $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;15;0.0176;1)=0.97206$. Difference $\approx 0.0009$ — tiny, because $N_a\ll N_p$.

title: Two-phase plan with a re-test window (binomial)
prompt:
A large batch (fraction out of tolerance **a = 0.04**) is inspected in two phases. **Phase 1**: control **N1 = 150** pieces — accept if the number out of tolerance is **≤ n11 = 3**; if it is between **n11+1** and **n12 = 8**, proceed to phase 2; otherwise reject. **Phase 2**: control **N2 = 400** pieces — accept if the number out of tolerance is **≤ n21 = 6**.

All counts are binomial (large batch). Compute the probability of acceptance in phase 1, the total probability of acceptance via phase 2, and the overall acceptance probability.
hint: Phase-1 accept = BINOM.DIST(n11; N1; a; 1). Reach phase 2 = BINOM.DIST(n12;N1;a;1)−BINOM.DIST(n11;N1;a;1). Phase-2 accept = BINOM.DIST(n21; N2; a; 1). Total = phase-1 + reach·phase-2.

given.A1: Two-phase acceptance plan (binomial)

checks.label: Accept in phase 1
checks.teach: Phase 1 accepts when at most $n_{11}=3$ of the 150 are out of tolerance: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(3;150;0.04;1)=0.1458$.

checks.label: Accept via phase 2
checks.teach: You **reach** phase 2 when phase-1 defectives fall in the window $(n_{11},\,n_{12}]=(3,8]$, whose probability is the difference of cumulatives $\text{BINOM.DIST}(8;150;0.04;1)-\text{BINOM.DIST}(3;150;0.04;1)$. You then **pass** phase 2 with probability $\text{BINOM.DIST}(6;400;0.04;1)$. Multiply: $0.00247$. The window probability is small because $a=0.04$ keeps most outcomes at or below 3.

checks.label: Total acceptance
checks.teach: The two accepting routes (accept in phase 1, or reach-and-pass phase 2) are mutually exclusive, so add: $0.1458+0.00247=0.1483$. Phase 2 adds little here, but it gives borderline batches a second chance rather than an outright rejection.

method: Phase-1 accept: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(3;150;0.04;1)=0.1458$. Reach phase 2 (window $3<X\le8$): $\text{BINOM.DIST}(8;150;0.04;1)-\text{BINOM.DIST}(3;150;0.04;1)$; pass it: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(6;400;0.04;1)$; product $=0.00247$. Total acceptance $=0.1458+0.00247=0.1483$.

title: Risk of the second kind β — data given as variances
prompt:
A process is **normal** with mean **m = 1.235** and **variance s² = 2.5·10⁻⁵**. A modification would shift it to mean **m₁ = 1.223** with variance **s₁² = 1.1·10⁻⁵**. At confidence level **P = 0.995** we accept a part as 'unchanged' if it lands in the **original** confidence interval. Compute the **risk of error of the second kind β**: the probability a part from the **new** process still falls in that interval.

The data are **variances**, so pass $\sqrt{s^2}$ as the standard deviation to every normal function. Build the original interval $[L,U]$ with NORM.INV, then $\beta=F_1(U)-F_1(L)$ using the new distribution's CDF.
hint: Use SQRT of each variance. L = NORM.INV((1−P)/2; m; SQRT(s²)); U = NORM.INV(1−(1−P)/2; m; SQRT(s²)). β = NORM.DIST(U;m1;SQRT(s1²);1) − NORM.DIST(L;m1;SQRT(s1²);1).

given.A1: β with variances — confidence P = 0.995
given.A7: Lower limit L of original interval
given.A8: Upper limit U of original interval

checks.label: Lower limit L
checks.teach: At $P=0.995$ each tail holds $(1-P)/2=0.0025$. The lower limit is that quantile of the **original** normal, with $\sigma=\sqrt{2.5\times10^{-5}}=0.005$: $L=\text{NORM.INV}(0.0025;1.235;0.005)=1.22096$. Converting the variance to a standard deviation with SQRT is essential.

checks.label: Upper limit U
checks.teach: Upper limit at the $0.9975$ quantile: $U=\text{NORM.INV}(0.9975;1.235;0.005)=1.24904$. The original 99.5 % interval is $[1.22096,\,1.24904]$.

checks.label: Risk β
checks.teach: β is the chance a part from the **new** process ($m_1=1.223$, $\sigma_1=\sqrt{1.1\times10^{-5}}=0.00332$) still lands in the old interval: $\beta=F_1(U)-F_1(L)=0.7303$. It is large here ($73\%$) because the new mean $1.223$ sits close to the lower limit $1.22096$ — the two distributions overlap heavily, so the modification is hard to detect. Again, both new-distribution CDFs use $\sqrt{s_1^2}$, not $s_1^2$.

method: Convert variances: $\sigma=\sqrt{2.5\times10^{-5}}=0.005$, $\sigma_1=\sqrt{1.1\times10^{-5}}=0.00332$. Original 99.5 % interval $L=\text{NORM.INV}(0.0025;1.235;0.005)=1.22096$, $U=\text{NORM.INV}(0.9975;1.235;0.005)=1.24904$. Then $\beta=F_1(U)-F_1(L)$ with the new $N(1.223,0.00332)$: $\beta=0.7303$ — a high type-II error because the distributions overlap strongly.

title: Two-component mechanism — failure risk and inverse design
prompt:
A mechanism uses **NA = 2** components of type A (each defective with probability **PA = 0.02**) and **NB = 3** of type B (each defective with probability **PB = 0.007**). It **fails if any** component is defective. Components are independent, so the chance that all of one type are good is the binomial mass at zero defectives.

Compute the probability that the mechanism does **not** work. Then, for the inverse design question, verify that lowering A's defect rate to **PA' = 0.015** brings the failure probability close to the target **P1 = 0.05**.
hint: P(all A good) = BINOM.DIST(0; NA; PA; 0). P(works) = P(all A good)·P(all B good). P(fails) = 1 − P(works). For the design check, recompute with PA' = 0.015.

given.A1: Two-component mechanism — failure risk

checks.label: P(all A good)
checks.teach: With $N_A=2$ independent components each defective with probability $0.02$, all good means zero defectives: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(0;2;0.02;0)=(1-0.02)^2=0.9604$. The cumulative flag 0 gives the exact mass at $k=0$.

checks.label: P(mechanism fails)
checks.teach: The mechanism works only if **both** types are fully good (independent), so $P(\text{works})=0.9604\times 0.97915=0.94037$, and the failure risk is $1-0.94037=0.05963$. Working through the complement avoids enumerating every defective combination.

checks.label: P(fails) with PA' = 0.015
checks.teach: Design question: to push the failure risk down to the target $P_1=0.05$, improve only type A. With $P_A'=0.015$: $P(\text{fails})=1-\text{BINOM.DIST}(0;2;0.015;0)\times 0.97915=0.05001$ — essentially the $0.05$ target. So tightening A's defect rate from $0.02$ to about $0.015$ achieves the goal, while B is left unchanged.

method: All-good probabilities: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(0;2;0.02;0)=0.9604$ and $\text{BINOM.DIST}(0;3;0.007;0)=0.97915$. $P(\text{works})=0.94037$, so $P(\text{fails})=0.05963$. For the inverse design, setting $P_A'=0.015$ gives $P(\text{fails})=1-\text{BINOM.DIST}(0;2;0.015;0)\cdot 0.97915=0.05001\approx P_1$.

title: Poisson approximation of an acceptance plan
prompt:
A batch of **nl = 3000** pieces is inspected by controlling **nc1 = 50** pieces; the batch is accepted if **nf1 = 1** or fewer are out of tolerance. The true fraction out of tolerance is **p = 0.02**. Compute the acceptance probability three ways — **exact hypergeometric**, **binomial** approximation, and **Poisson** approximation — and the difference between the exact and the Poisson values.

The Poisson rate is $\lambda=n_{c1}\,p$. Poisson approximates the binomial when $n$ is large and $p$ small.
hint: Exact = HYPGEOM.DIST(nf1; nc1; p·nl; nl; 1). Binomial = BINOM.DIST(nf1; nc1; p; 1). Poisson = POISSON.DIST(nf1; nc1·p; 1). Difference = exact − Poisson.

given.A1: Acceptance probability — exact vs binomial vs Poisson

checks.label: Exact (hypergeometric)
checks.teach: The batch holds $p\cdot n_l=60$ defectives. Drawing 50 without replacement, 'accept if $\le 1$' is $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;50;60;3000;1)=0.73584$ — the exact value.

checks.label: Approximate (binomial)
checks.teach: Treating draws as independent with $p=0.02$: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;50;0.02;1)=0.73577$. Very close, since $50\ll 3000$.

checks.label: Approximate (Poisson)
checks.teach: The Poisson approximation uses the single rate $\lambda=n_{c1}\,p=50\times 0.02=1$: $\text{POISSON.DIST}(1;1;1)=0.73576$. Poisson stands in for the binomial when $n$ is large and $p$ small (the 'law of rare events'); here $\lambda=1$.

checks.label: Difference exact − Poisson
checks.teach: Exact minus Poisson is only $\approx 8.4\times10^{-5}$ — far below $0.01\%$ would require a larger batch, but it already shows all three models agree to four decimals. The cascade hypergeometric → binomial → Poisson trades a little accuracy for a much simpler single-parameter formula.

method: Defectives $=p\cdot n_l=60$. Exact: $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(1;50;60;3000;1)=0.73584$. Binomial: $\text{BINOM.DIST}(1;50;0.02;1)=0.73577$. Poisson ($\lambda=n_{c1}p=1$): $\text{POISSON.DIST}(1;1;1)=0.73576$. Exact − Poisson $\approx 8.4\times10^{-5}$ — all three agree closely because $n_{c1}\ll n_l$ and $p$ is small.

title: Separation limit with a rectangular measurement error
prompt:
Two part types must be separated by mass: type T1 has nominal mass **m1 = 12.32 g**, type T2 has **m2 = 12.36 g**. The scale has a **maximum error E = 0.01 g**, and with no other information the error is assumed **uniform (rectangular)**. Find the selection limit **L** such that the risk of accepting a T2 part as T1 is **R = 0.001**.

A rectangular error of half-width $E$ has variance $\sigma^2=E^2/3$. Since $m_2>m_1$ and we want T2 parts wrongly read **below** L with probability $R$ (a left-tail risk on the T2 distribution), $L=\text{NORM.INV}(R;\,m_2;\,\sqrt{E^2/3})$.
hint: Variance σ² = E²/3 (rectangular). L = NORM.INV(R; m2; SQRT(σ²)). Also compute the 95 % upper limit of m1 for comparison.

given.A1: Separation limit with a rectangular (uniform) error
given.A7: Variance of the measurement  σ² = E²/3

checks.label: Measurement variance σ²
checks.teach: A uniform (rectangular) distribution of half-width $E$ has variance $\sigma^2=E^2/3=0.01^2/3=3.333\times10^{-5}\,\text{g}^2$ (standard deviation $\sigma=0.00577$ g). The maximum error is the **half**-interval of total variability; the $/3$ is the variance of a uniform distribution. The error distribution is rectangular even though the measured quantity is treated as normal.

checks.label: Upper limit of m1 at 95 %
checks.teach: For reference, the two-sided 95 % upper limit of an $m_1$ measurement is $\text{NORM.INV}(0.975;12.32;\sqrt{\sigma^2})=12.3313$ g — still below $L$, confirming a T1 part rarely reads above the selection limit.

checks.label: Selection limit L
checks.teach: Since $m_2>m_1$, a T2 part is misclassified as T1 only if its measured mass falls **below** $L$. Requiring that left-tail risk to equal $R=0.001$ on the T2 distribution gives $L=\text{NORM.INV}(0.001;12.36;0.00577)=12.3422$ g. Parts measuring below $12.3422$ g are called T1; the chance a true T2 slips under is exactly $0.1\%$.

method: A rectangular error of half-width $E$ has variance $\sigma^2=E^2/3=3.333\times10^{-5}$ g² ($\sigma=0.00577$ g). The risk $R$ is a left-tail (type-II) risk on the heavier T2 distribution, so $L=\text{NORM.INV}(R;m_2;\sigma)=\text{NORM.INV}(0.001;12.36;0.00577)=12.3422$ g. The 95 % upper limit of $m_1$ is $12.3313$ g, comfortably below $L$.

title: Pearson χ² — tail probabilities and inverse
prompt:
A test statistic follows a **chi-squared (Pearson) distribution** with **ν = 5** degrees of freedom. Using **CHISQ.DIST(x; ν; cumulative)** (cumulative = 1 gives the left tail $P(\chi^2\le x)$) and the inverse **CHISQ.INV(P; ν)**, answer the questions.

The χ² distribution is **not symmetric** (it lives on $x\ge 0$ and is right-skewed), so left- and right-tail boundaries are not mirror images.
hint: Right tail = 1 − CHISQ.DIST(x;ν;1). Interval = F(b)−F(a). Lower boundary = CHISQ.INV(αi;ν); upper boundary = CHISQ.INV(1−αs;ν).

given.A1: Pearson χ², ν = 5 degrees of freedom

checks.label: P(χ² < 3.5)
checks.teach: With cumulative = 1, $\text{CHISQ.DIST}(3.5;5;1)=F(3.5)=0.3766$ is the left-tail probability $P(\chi^2\le 3.5)$ — read straight off the CDF, just like the normal.

checks.label: P(χ² > 22.3)
checks.teach: The right tail is the complement: $P(\chi^2>22.3)=1-F(22.3)=0.00046$. A value this far out in the upper tail (with only 5 d.o.f.) is very unlikely — the χ² right tail is what variance tests use to flag an unexpectedly large spread.

checks.label: P(7.3 < χ² < 18.2)
checks.teach: An interval probability is the difference of CDFs: $F(18.2)-F(7.3)=0.9973-0.8007=0.1966$. Subtract the lower from the upper, never add.

checks.label: Lower boundary χ²i
checks.teach: CHISQ.INV inverts the left-tail CDF: $\text{CHISQ.INV}(0.01;5)=0.5543$ is the value below which only 1 % of the distribution lies. Because χ² is positive and skewed, this lower boundary sits close to 0.

checks.label: Upper boundary χ²s
checks.teach: For an **upper** boundary leaving right-tail risk $\alpha_s=0.01$, feed the left-cumulative $1-\alpha_s=0.99$: $\text{CHISQ.INV}(0.99;5)=15.086$. Note how far the upper boundary (15.09) is from the lower (0.55) — the asymmetry of χ² means the two tails are nowhere near symmetric about the mean ($=\nu=5$).

method: Left tail: $F(x)=\text{CHISQ.DIST}(x;\nu;1)$, e.g. $F(3.5)=0.3766$. Right tail: $1-F(22.3)=0.00046$. Interval: $F(18.2)-F(7.3)=0.1966$. Inverse boundaries: $\chi^2_i=\text{CHISQ.INV}(0.01;5)=0.554$ and $\chi^2_s=\text{CHISQ.INV}(0.99;5)=15.086$ — strongly asymmetric because χ² is right-skewed.

title: Fisher F — tail probabilities and inverse
prompt:
A variance-ratio statistic follows a **Fisher F distribution** with **m = 7** numerator and **n = 5** denominator degrees of freedom. Using **F.DIST(f; m; n; cumulative)** (cumulative = 1 gives the left tail $P(F\le f)$) and the inverse **F.INV(P; m; n)**, answer the questions.

The F distribution is right-skewed and depends on **two** degrees-of-freedom parameters (order matters: numerator first, then denominator).
hint: Right tail = 1 − F.DIST(f;m;n;1). Interval = F(b)−F(a). Lower boundary = F.INV(αi;m;n); upper boundary = F.INV(1−αs;m;n).

given.A1: Fisher F, m = 7 (num.), n = 5 (den.) degrees of freedom

checks.label: P(F > 1.3)
checks.teach: The right tail is the complement of the CDF: $P(F>1.3)=1-F(1.3)=0.3996$. The F right tail is the basis of the variance-ratio (ANOVA / two-variance) test.

checks.label: P(F < 2.6)
checks.teach: A 'less than' probability reads off the cumulative directly: $\text{F.DIST}(2.6;7;5;1)=F(2.6)=0.8449$. Note the argument order: value, numerator d.o.f., denominator d.o.f., cumulative.

checks.label: P(1.8 < F < 2.3)
checks.teach: Interval probability = $F(2.3)-F(1.8)=0.8119-0.7320=0.0799$. As always, subtract the lower CDF from the upper.

checks.label: Lower boundary fi
checks.teach: F.INV inverts the left-tail CDF: $\text{F.INV}(0.01;7;5)=0.1340$ is the value with 1 % of the distribution below it. F is positive and skewed, so the lower boundary is well below 1.

checks.label: Upper boundary fs
checks.teach: For an **upper** boundary leaving right-tail risk $\alpha_s=0.02$, feed the left-cumulative $1-\alpha_s=0.98$: $\text{F.INV}(0.98;7;5)=7.614$. The large gap between the lower (0.13) and upper (7.61) boundaries reflects the strong right skew of F with these small degrees of freedom.

method: Left tail: $F(f)=\text{F.DIST}(f;m;n;1)$, e.g. $F(2.6)=0.8449$. Right tail: $1-F(1.3)=0.3996$. Interval: $F(2.3)-F(1.8)=0.0799$. Inverse boundaries: $f_i=\text{F.INV}(0.01;7;5)=0.134$ and $f_s=\text{F.INV}(0.98;7;5)=7.614$. Keep the numerator d.o.f. ($m=7$) before the denominator ($n=5$).
